turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Good morning, this is The Morning Commute. Thursday, July ninth. I'm Sam.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: And I'm Alex. The throughline today is a full reversal of where we left off two weeks ago. Back then, cheaper oil and lower yields were doing the Fed's work — the AI trade as a disinflation trade. That flipped, hard. Warsh's Fed, in its first-ever minutes, named AI-infrastructure electricity demand as an upside inflation risk. The Iran ceasefire collapsed overnight and shoved oil and the long end of the curve straight back up. Rate cuts have drained out of 2026 entirely. And yet — semis still led the tape.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So the same force is on both sides of the ledger now — it's the cause of the inflation and the reason to buy the market.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's the whole show. The AI buildout is what the central bank is pointing at as inflationary, and it's also the only growth engine anyone wants to own. The trade stopped being the cure and became the disease — and the market is buying the disease with both hands.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: A market long the very thing the Fed is worried about. Let's get into it.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Headlines. World desk first, and there's one place to start — Iran, the engine under everything today.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The June eighteenth framework is dead. At the NATO summit in Turkey, President Trump declared the ceasefire over after Iran hit three commercial ships in the Strait of Hormuz and struck US military sites in the Gulf. The US ran two waves — roughly eighty targets across Iran Tuesday, ninety more overnight into Thursday. Iran answered: strikes on US positions in Bahrain and Kuwait, about ten ballistic missiles at a US base in northern Jordan — intercepted — and Qatar targeted. Second straight day of exchanges this morning.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So a war premium is back in oil, but the market isn't pricing Armageddon.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's exactly the distinction, and it's the whole trade. Trump added he doesn't want a full-scale war and left the door open to talks — Oxford Economics reads it as on-and-off, escalate then de-escalate, rather than all-out. Two weeks ago on this show we called the leftover war premium cheap optionality in the tails — a framework with a Senate fight and an inspections dispute attached, not a signed treaty. Well, the tail just paid off. The optionality was cheap for a reason.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And unlike the spring flare-up, this one has the market's full attention on the one chokepoint that matters.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Hormuz. Roughly a fifth of the world's oil moves through that strait. You don't need a single tanker actually sunk — you need the insurance and freight desks to reprice the risk, and they did, instantly. Which is why crude gapped on a headline and yields followed. Everything downstream today flows from this one story.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Second world story — Europe's summer as its own risk.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: A fast-moving wildfire is tearing through the Pyrenees foothills, more than ten thousand people evacuated — the early, punishing heat season reading straight through to power demand and public health, the same grid-stress theme we keep flagging. And on the politics wire, Marine Le Pen says she'll run for the 2027 French presidency despite an embezzlement sentence and a court-ordered electronic monitor. National Rally leads the polls with Macron term-limited — a reminder that European political risk is quietly building under the market's nose while everyone stares at Washington and Tehran.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Good guardrail. Let's do the tape — the reversal is written all over it.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Wednesday's close was a split screen. The S&P off three-tenths of a percent to seventy-four eighty-two, down twenty-one points. The Nasdaq up two-tenths to twenty-five-eight-seventy. But the Dow down one-and-one-tenth percent — five hundred seventy-seven points — to fifty-two-three-forty-eight. And the Russell 2000 off nearly nine-tenths.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: The exact opposite of the June rotation. Two weeks ago the Dow held green while the Nasdaq bled and we called it value taking the baton. Now the Dow is the one bleeding and tech is bid.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Complete reversal, and it's the same variable running the other way. In June, falling oil and falling yields lifted the rate-sensitive, old-economy names — the Dow's industrials, financials, the homebuilders. Now oil and yields are ripping higher, so those same names get sold, and money crowds back into the one place that doesn't need cheap money to compound — the AI semiconductors.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Quantify the oil move, because it's the input to all of it.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Brent settled seventy-eight-oh-two, up five-point-two percent — nearly four dollars on the session — and touched seventy-nine intraday. WTI back around seventy-five, from the low seventies. Both jumped the instant Trump said the ceasefire was over. This morning they're giving a little back, Brent near seventy-six — but the level has reset higher and, more importantly, the two-way volatility is back in the tape after a month of it bleeding out.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the bond move, which is really the headline for equities.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The long end led it, and that's the tell. The ten-year closed four-point-five-six percent, up from four-forty-eight just two sessions earlier — an eight-basis-point backup. The two-year four-twenty-one. And the thirty-year pushed back above five percent, to five-oh-six, the twenty-year at five-oh-seven. That's a bear-steepener: the long end selling off faster than the front. And the distinction matters — this isn't the market pricing more Fed hikes at the front end. It's the market repricing inflation and term premium at the back end. Mortgage rates jumped right alongside it.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the Fed lit that fuse. The minutes were the other bombshell.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The other bombshell, and the heart of the throughline. Wednesday brought the minutes from Warsh's first meeting, June sixteen-seventeen. They held twelve-to-nothing at three-and-a-half to three-three-quarters. But the language turned hawkish, and the reason is genuinely new. Quote: "ongoing strong demand for AI infrastructure would likely sustain upward pressure on prices for technology products and electricity." They said AI capex "showed no signs of slowing" and keeps exceeding expectations. Many participants now see the appropriate funds rate ending the year above the current range — meaning a hike is live.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So the central bank has, in writing, blamed the AI boom for inflation.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: In writing. Half of the eighteen dots favored a hike by year-end. And here's the Warsh signature: he submitted no dot of his own, and the statement ran about a hundred thirty words — half the usual length. Less forward guidance, more flexibility, more optionality for the chair. The market got the message: futures have gone from pricing multiple 2026 cuts a month ago to essentially zero now.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And yet volatility stayed contained, which tells you what kind of move this is.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It tells you it's a repricing, not a panic. The VIX closed under seventeen. Even gold fell — down two-point-two percent to forty-eighty-two, and remember, gold is well off its January record near fifty-three-hundred and actually down on the year. When real yields back up this fast, even the classic haven wobbles. So this is cold, deliberate reallocation: sell duration, sell the rate-sensitives, buy the AI compounders, and respect the oil tail. Under the hood it's orderly — which is arguably what makes it more durable than a fear spike.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: US business — lead with the group that actually led.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The semiconductors, and the standout is AMD — up seven-and-three-quarters percent Thursday. Two catalysts. One, a prominent Street call that the recent tech-and-memory selloff had finally bottomed — a green light for long-term buyers to step back in. Two, a Morgan Stanley supply-chain report flagging that AMD's advanced-packaging allocation is set to rise sharply through 2027 to meet infrastructure demand. The Philadelphia semiconductor index is up about forty-seven percent year-to-date, and the AI-chip complex has added something like two trillion dollars of market value this year alone.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the twist — the biggest name in the group is the one lagging.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The great irony of this rally. Nvidia has become the black sheep of the chip trade — still seventy to eighty percent of AI-GPU share, still the franchise, but underperforming the group as money spreads out to the second-line names: AMD, the advanced-packaging and memory suppliers, the networking plays. When the leader lags a rally in its own sector, that's classic late-cycle rotation inside a theme — investors reaching for whoever hasn't run yet, chasing the second derivative.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Is that a warning sign or a healthy broadening?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Depends which bear you ask. The bull read is healthy broadening — the AI trade maturing from one name into a whole supply chain. The bear read, and it was making the rounds this week, is that it rhymes with 1999: semis are now around a fifth of US market cap, tech-related names more than half. For scale: 2026 chip sales are tracking near nine hundred seventy-five billion dollars, gen-AI chips alone around five hundred billion. Concentration like that cuts both ways, and everyone knows it.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Second US story — a reality check on the other AI frontier, autonomy.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: A hard one. The regulator, NHTSA, called robotaxis a "danger to the public." The administrator, Jonathan Morrison, cited a "clear pattern" of autonomous vehicles interfering with first responders — driving into active emergency scenes, blocking ambulances and fire crews, failing to read flares, smoke and cones. He gave the industry until the end of July to present fixes. Waymo alone had at least six documented incidents through March, including one where an officer had to physically move a Waymo out of the way at a Dallas gas-explosion scene in June.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: The second-order read for the AV names?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Regulatory risk just got concrete for Waymo, Tesla's robotaxi push and Zoox. Their commercial models assume permissionless, city-by-city expansion — that's how the growth math works. A federal "functional insufficiency" finding, with a deadline attached, is exactly the kind of thing that slows a rollout and reprices the timeline the market has been paying up for. The technology is scaling into cities faster than the rule-making, and this is the first real shot across the bow.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Two international stories, and they both come back to bond markets.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Start with Japan, because it may be the single most important chart in the world right now. New Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi is pushing a pro-growth agenda — talk of something like ten trillion yen a year in fiscal expansion, spending and tax cuts. The bond market is recoiling. The ten-year JGB yield hit around two-point-eight-five percent — the highest since roughly 1996, a thirty-year high — and it's marching toward three.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the yen went the other way, which is the puzzle again.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The same puzzle as June, only more extreme. Dollar-yen pushed past a hundred sixty-two, near the weakest yen since the mid-1980s. You'd expect higher JGB yields to firm the currency. But when the yields are rising because investors fear fiscal dominance — deficits so large the central bank may have to finance them — that's bearish for the currency, not bullish. The Bank of Japan is boxed: hike to defend the yen and you blow up the government's debt-service math; sit still and the yen keeps sliding.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And why should a US listener care about a Japanese government bond yield?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Because it's a coded warning for US stocks. Japanese institutions are collectively the largest foreign holder of US assets on the planet. For twenty years, near-zero yields at home pushed that money into US Treasuries and equities. When they can finally get two-and-a-half, closing on three percent, risk-free, in their own currency, the incentive to keep that capital in America weakens. A rising JGB yield is a slow, structural pull of global capital back toward Tokyo — it's the yen-carry fault line we've flagged before, now with a fiscal accelerant underneath it.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Second international — the deal-making boom, which is its own tell on liquidity.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: A big one. Morgan Stanley now sees global mergers and acquisitions hitting a record six-point-four trillion dollars in 2026, eclipsing the 2021 mania. Second-quarter announced deals were up sixty-four percent year-on-year, led by software, utilities, energy and healthcare. The drivers: a friendlier antitrust posture, something like four-point-three trillion in private-equity dry powder waiting to be deployed, and a reviving IPO window.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Reconcile that with a Fed that's threatening to hike. Cheap money is supposed to be the fuel for M&A.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's the tension, and it's instructive. Deal-making at this pace, into rising rates, tells you corporates and sponsors are choosing consolidation as the growth strategy precisely when organic growth is scarce and financing windows feel like they could slam shut. And look at the sector table: utilities and energy near the top — that's the power-and-data-center land grab showing up directly in the M&A tape. Worth flagging the dispersion, though — PwC's estimate is nearer four trillion. Same direction, very different magnitude, so take the exact number with a pinch of salt.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: China to close the block — and it's the AI arms race, funded.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The Chinese AI labs are raising war chests. Zhipu AI launched a four-billion-dollar Hong Kong share placement Wednesday, after a roughly fifteen-hundred-percent run since its January listing — a hundred-twelve-billion-dollar valuation now. And DeepSeek raised more than seven-point-four billion in its first outside round last month, valuing it north of fifty billion. This is China matching the hyperscaler capex logic — you cannot compete in frontier AI without a balance sheet, and they've decided to build one.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the fresh angle here isn't the fundraising, it's what they're doing with it.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Right — this is the genuinely new development. Both are moving toward custom in-house silicon. DeepSeek is building its own inference chip to cut reliance on Nvidia and Huawei. Zhipu is in early talks on a custom ASIC for its models after a twenty-seven-fold surge in usage. It's the vertical-integration playbook — the same one Google ran with its TPUs, and Amazon with Trainium — now going Chinese.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: The second-order read on the decoupling scoreboard?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It cuts both ways, and that's the nuance people miss. Designing your own chip is a sovereignty move — it routes around export controls on the demand side, on what you're allowed to buy. But they're still boxed on the supply side. US limits on advanced foundry access and on high-bandwidth memory mean they can design a world-class ASIC on paper but struggle to actually fabricate it at the leading edge, or to feed it the HBM it needs to run. So you get elite model labs, flush with cash, designing silicon they can't fully build. The ambition has outrun the supply chain — which is exactly what the export controls were engineered to do. The race is real; the ceiling is also real.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Perfect place to pivot. Take it to the energy desk — because that's where this whole inflation story actually lives.

segments.raw: SEGMENT TWO — ENERGY / DATA CENTERS / UTILITIES
segments.label: Energy / Data Centers / Utilities

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Segment two — energy, data centers and the grid. And Alex, today this isn't a side segment. The Fed just made it the macro story.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: They did, and that reframes the whole desk. The single most important line in those FOMC minutes wasn't about tariffs or Iran — it was that AI-infrastructure demand would "sustain upward pressure on prices for technology products and electricity." The central bank has formally wired the data-center buildout into the inflation outlook. The mechanism we've been describing on this show for weeks is now in the Fed's own risk case: capex, to power demand, to electricity prices, to sticky goods inflation, to higher-for-longer — which is precisely why the thirty-year is back above five percent.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So let's put the capex number on it, because that's the top of the chain.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The big five hyperscalers are tracking somewhere around seven hundred to nine hundred billion dollars of capex combined for 2026 — up roughly thirty-six percent year-on-year. Amazon near two hundred billion, Alphabet a hundred seventy-five to one-eighty-five, Meta just raised its range to a hundred twenty-five to a hundred forty-five — and explicitly cited higher data-center and component costs as the reason for the raise. Microsoft north of a hundred twenty, Oracle around fifty. When Meta tells you the buildout is costing more than it planned, that is the inflation the Fed is pointing at, showing up in a single earnings line.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the bottleneck you keep saying is the real constraint — where is it binding hardest right now?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The gas turbine. GE Vernova is sitting on something like a hundred gigawatts of turbine reservations and backlog — effectively sold out through 2030, already contracting into 2031. And roughly a fifth of that is data-center load specifically. Their electrification arm booked two-point-four billion dollars of data-center equipment orders in a single quarter — more than all of last year combined. You can announce a gigawatt campus tomorrow; you cannot conjure a turbine slot before 2030. That lead time has become the binding constraint — arguably tighter than the chips themselves at this point.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the deals are getting creative to get around it. Give me the marquee ones.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Three that matter, and notice the pattern in them. Chevron and Microsoft signed a twenty-year agreement for dedicated on-site natural-gas generation for a campus near Pecos, Texas — an oil major becoming a merchant power provider to a cloud company, bypassing the grid entirely. Digital Realty bought fourteen hundred acres in Kansas City with a deal for six hundred megawatts by early 2028, scaling to two gigawatts. And SoftBank committed up to seventy-five billion euros — about eighty-five billion dollars — for five gigawatts of AI data centers in France, with a three-point-one-gigawatt first phase in the industrial north by 2031.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the nuclear leg, because that's the long-dated bet everyone's making.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Moving, but slow. FERC approved a transmission waiver on June first that shifts seven hundred sixty megawatts of grid-connection rights over to Crane — the old Three Mile Island Unit One — clearing the last grid obstacle for a full restart in 2027. And Meta signed with Oklo for one-point-two gigawatts in Ohio. But here's the sober read: none of that closes the near-term gap. Restarts are 2027, small modular reactors are the 2030s. What actually feeds the load this decade is gas, and keeping existing plants — including coal — online longer than anyone planned. The clean-and-nuclear timeline is simply out of phase with the AI timeline.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Now bring it to the kitchen table, because this is where your macro and someone's actual power bill finally meet.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: This is the part that turns it political — and it's the throughline's sharpest edge. PJM, the biggest US grid, sixty-seven million people, just started its new capacity delivery year on June first at three hundred twenty-nine dollars per megawatt-day. Two years ago that number was under twenty-nine dollars. The following year cleared at the cap. Translate it into a bill: an extra sixteen to eighteen dollars a month on residential customers right now, an estimated seventy a month by 2028, and roughly a hundred billion dollars in added capacity costs across PJM through 2033.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So the data center and the household electric bill are now literally the same line item.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: They are. And that is exactly what the Fed flagged as inflationary. When a ratepayer in Ohio or western Maryland opens a bill that jumped to underwrite a hyperscaler's load, the cross-subsidization fight goes political fast — state regulators, consumer advocates, the whole fight over who pays for the interconnection. And critically for the macro: that electricity component keeps feeding the very core inflation the central bank is now hiking against. Oil and yields you can argue are cyclical — they move both ways. This power-cost impulse is structural, multi-year, and it points in one direction. That's why the energy desk isn't a segment today. It's the transmission mechanism of the entire market.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Beautifully put. Take it to the Philippines — where the same oil shock lands, but completely inverted.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Segment three — the Philippines. Weather lead, then the macro, and the macro ties straight back to the oil we opened on.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Weather first. Super Typhoon Inday — international name Bavi — is a genuine monster out at sea: sustained winds around a hundred ninety-five kilometers an hour, gusts to two hundred forty. Signal Number One is up over twelve areas in Northern Luzon — Batanes, the Babuyan Islands, parts of Cagayan, Isabela and Abra. And the good news is real and worth stating plainly: it is not expected to make landfall. It stays offshore, weakens through today, exits the Philippine area by Saturday and heads for eastern China this weekend.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: But the wind was never really the story here, was it. It's the rain.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It's the rain, again — the exact pattern we saw with Francisco two weeks ago. Inday is pulling in and supercharging the southwest monsoon — the habagat — so even with the storm's center way out at sea, you get heavy, soaking rain across Luzon, including Metro Manila, and into the western Visayas and Mindanao, July eighth through tenth. Manila already saw gutter-deep flooding on Taft and T.M. Kalaw after one downpour. A near-miss on the wind, a very real soaking from the monsoon.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Now the macro, and there's genuinely good news buried in it — which is where it gets interesting.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: There is. The central bank, the BSP, held the policy rate at four-and-three-quarters percent — that's after back-to-back hikes in April and June, fifty basis points of tightening to defend the currency and cap imported inflation. And June inflation cooled to six-point-four percent, down from six-point-eight in May — the second straight monthly slowdown, and back inside their own forecast band. The driver of the improvement: transport inflation fell to twelve-point-eight percent from over sixteen, because oil had been falling through June.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: "Had been." And that's the whole problem, isn't it.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's the entire problem. Every good number in that Philippine print is the June oil relief flowing through with a lag — and we just spent twenty minutes explaining how that relief violently reversed this week. So the tailwind that cooled their inflation, firmed their peso, and made one more hike optional, is now — as of Wednesday — a fresh headwind pointed straight at all three. And there's a warning already sitting in the detail: core inflation actually rose to four-point-four percent, still above the two-to-four target. The headline is improving; the sticky core underneath is not.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Put the peso in context, because that's the forcing function for them.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Around sixty-one-and-a-half to the dollar — but note, that's off the record lows near sixty-two they hit back in April. The peso had actually firmed on the cooler inflation and the softer crude. The vulnerability underneath is the trade gap: May's deficit ballooned to five-and-a-half billion dollars, imports up nearly twenty-two percent, oil-driven — reportedly the worst trade balance in ASEAN. For a net oil importer, a fresh war premium in crude widens that gap and pressures the currency all over again, mechanically.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the structural offsets that usually cushion them?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Still there, and still the reason it's a squeeze and not a crisis. Remittances from overseas Filipino workers run close to a tenth of GDP — a record thirty-five-plus billion dollars last year — and a weak peso actually flatters those in local-currency terms. Plus the business-process-outsourcing sector earning dollars. Those are the shock absorbers. But absorbers get tested, and a sustained oil spike tests them right where it hurts — the import bill and the household budget.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So the throughline lands inverted on them.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Perfectly inverted. For the US, this AI-and-oil story is an inflation-and-rates problem. For the Philippines, it's an imported-inflation, weak-peso, widening-deficit problem — the same barrel of crude, felt on the household budget instead of the bond market. In June they got real breathing room. This week that breathing room is on notice. Governor Remolona has said the economy could absorb one more hike if needed — and the Iran flare-up just made "if needed" a great deal more likely into their August meeting.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Cyclically on the back foot again, structurally intact. On to vocab.

segments.raw: SEGMENT FOUR — VOCAB OF THE DAY
segments.label: Vocab Of The Day

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Segment four — vocab. Two Mandarin, two Tagalog, each tied to a story in today's show, and we do the whole thing in-language. Mandarin first.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: 第一个词是高级连接词 —— 一旦。(The first word is an advanced connective — yīdàn, "once," or "in the event that.") 一旦霍尔木兹海峡受到威胁，油价就会立刻上涨。(Once the Strait of Hormuz is threatened, oil prices jump immediately.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: 一旦。这正是今天的石油故事。那它跟 如果 有什么区别?(Yīdàn. That's exactly today's oil story. So how is it different from rúguǒ, "if"?)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: 如果 是中性的假设 —— 也许会，也许不会。(Rúguǒ is a neutral hypothetical — maybe it will, maybe it won't.) 一旦 更有分量 —— 它假定事情真的会发生，而且一发生，后果就无法避免。(Yīdàn is weightier — it assumes the thing really will happen, and once it does, the consequence can't be avoided.) 搭配是 一旦……就……，语气偏书面、偏正式，是新闻和财经的语言。(The pattern is yīdàn… jiù…; the register leans written and formal — it's the language of news and finance.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: 一旦 —— 一发生，就来真的。第二个词。(Yīdàn — once it happens, it's for real. Second word.)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: 第二个词是 估计 —— "to estimate," or "to reckon." 市场估计今年不会降息了。(The second word is gūjì. The market reckons there'll be no rate cuts this year.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: 估计。那跟财经新闻里常见的 预计 呢?(Gūjì. And what about yùjì, which you see all over financial news?)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: 这就是重点 —— 语域的差别。估计 更口语，是日常的猜测、大概的判断。(That's the key — a difference in register. Gūjì is more colloquial — an everyday guess, a rough judgment.) 预计 更正式、更书面 —— 是机构、是分析师用的 "forecast"。(Yùjì is more formal and written — the "forecast" an institution or an analyst uses.) 你跟朋友说 估计，财经报告里写 预计。同样的意思，不同的场合。(You say gūjì to a friend; a finance report writes yùjì. Same meaning, different setting.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: 估计 是随口一说，预计 是白纸黑字。清楚了。现在换他加禄语。(Gūjì is off-the-cuff, yùjì is in black and white. Got it. Now to Tagalog.)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Tagalog naman, at para sa mga katutubong tainga, kaya't diretso tayo sa nuance. Unang salita: pasanin. (Tagalog now, and for native ears, so let's go straight to the nuance. First word: pasanin — a burden one shoulders.) Ang import bill sa langis at ang trade deficit — iyon ang pasanin ng piso ngayon. (The oil-import bill and the trade deficit — that's the peso's burden right now.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Pasanin. Mula sa "pasan" — ang dinadala mo sa balikat. At ang kaibahan sa "pabigat"? (From "pasan" — what you carry on your shoulders. And the contrast with "pabigat"?)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Ang pasanin ay responsibilidad na binubuhat mo nang may tapang. (A pasanin is a responsibility you take on and carry, often with resolve.) Ang pabigat ay dagdag na bigat na humihila pababa — parang bato sa bulsa. (A pabigat is added dead-weight that drags you down — like a stone in your pocket.) Ang pasanin ay pinapasan; ang pabigat ay pasakit lang. Magkaiba ang damdamin. (A pasanin you bear; a pabigat is just a hindrance. A completely different feeling.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Malinaw. Pangalawang salita — at bumalik tayo sa panahon. (Clear. Second word — and back to the weather.)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Pangalawa: sigwa —— unos, bagyo, tempestad. (Second: sigwa — a storm, a squall, a tempest.) Pero ang ganda ng salita, may matalinghagang kahulugan din —— sigwa ng damdamin, sigwa sa pulitika. (But the beauty of the word — it also has a figurative sense: a storm of emotion, a storm in politics.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Kaya perpekto ito ngayon. (So it's perfect for today.)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Oo —— si Bagyong Inday sa dagat, at ang sigwa sa merkado at sa Gitnang Silangan. (Yes — Typhoon Inday out at sea, and the sigwa in the markets and the Middle East.) Ang kaibahan sa "bagyo": ang bagyo ay ang literal na typhoon sa mapa; ang sigwa ay ang unos na pwede ring sa puso, sa lipunan, sa buhay. (The contrast with "bagyo": a bagyo is the literal typhoon on the map; sigwa is the tempest that can also be of the heart, of society, of life.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Bagyo sa langit, sigwa sa puso. Dalawang Mandarin, dalawang Tagalog, lahat mula sa balita ngayon. (A typhoon in the sky, a tempest in the heart. Two Mandarin, two Tagalog, all from today's news.)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Iyan ang ideya. Language inside the news. (That's the idea. Language inside the news.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Segment five — art and pop, quick.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Pop royalty first. Madonna dropped "Confessions Two" last Friday — her fifteenth album, and a direct sequel to 2005's "Confessions on a Dance Floor." She reunited with producer Stuart Price, and the guest list is a statement: Sabrina Carpenter, Feid, Stromae, and her daughter Lola Leon. An EDM-leaning record that critics are calling her best in about twenty years.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: A sequel, twenty years on — that's the same nostalgia instinct we keep bumping into.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It is — a legacy artist returning deliberately to the sound that defined her peak. And on the rock side, Jack White releases "Frozen Charlotte" tomorrow on Third Man — his seventh solo album, heavy-blues and seventies-riff, with singles like "God and the Broken Ribs" and "Dollar Bill." Two icons, two returns to form on the same weekend.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Anything at the box office worth a line?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: One, and it's a cautionary tale. Illumination's "Minions and Monsters" opened the July Fourth weekend to about thirty-six million dollars domestic — a franchise worst, below even the original "Despicable Me" from 2010. When even the reliable animation money-machine sputters, the read across all of this is the same: audiences are reaching for the familiar, and the familiar itself is getting harder to sell.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Comfort culture, again. Good cue for the meme.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Segment six — the meme, reported straight.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: And it ties right into the whole show, which is honestly why it's here. The format going around is "the extreme outlier ChatGPT user." A widely shared research finding on how people use AI for creative writing flagged one anonymized user as a statistical "extreme outlier" — someone who had generated thousands upon thousands of AI writing prompts, orders of magnitude beyond any normal user. Screenshots of that "extreme outlier" line spread across the feeds almost instantly.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And why did it catch the way it did?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Because it's become shorthand for how strangely, obsessively deep some people are going with these tools — the single data point so far off the chart it breaks the graph. It's the internet laughing at the sheer scale of AI adoption, right at the moment the Fed is blaming that same adoption for inflation. The joke and the macro story are, quite literally, the same phenomenon. There's a quieter one doing numbers too — the "Twins at the Carousel" animation clip, a deadpan character staring into the camera while everything spins around their head. But the outlier one is the through-note: even the memes are about AI now.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: The feed metabolizing the macro. Let's end on something good.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: One good thing to close on.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: A genuinely uplifting one, and it's a slow-burn kind of win — the sort that never gets a headline. Europe's air is measurably cleaner. The EU's Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service reported this week that sulphur-dioxide and nitrogen-oxide emissions have been falling roughly three to five percent every single year since 2010, and transport-sector carbon dioxide dropped to one-point-oh-five billion tonnes in 2024, down from one-point-one billion in 2019.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: After a whole segment on power bills and turbines, a story about emissions actually falling lands differently.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It really does. The director of the monitoring service, Laurence Rouil, called it "steady progress in improving air quality" — and that's exactly the point. No single dramatic breakthrough, just a continent quietly, measurably breathing easier year after year. The unglamorous kind of progress that compounds — cleaner lungs, fewer hospital days, a better sky over some of the most crowded cities on earth. Easy to miss, and worth noticing.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: A little good news under a heavy tape. We'll take it.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's your morning. The AI trade became the inflation trade, oil and the long end are back in charge, and the buildout underneath it just won't quit — that's the shape of the day.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Wherever this finds you, take a breath, take it easy, and we'll be right back here tomorrow morning.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: See you then.
